title: Pre-trained Language Models can be Fully Zero-Shot Learners

structured.summary: This paper proposes NPPrompt, a fully zero-shot prompting method that automatically induces verbalizers by leveraging pretrained language model static embeddings to find semantically related label words, aggregating masked token predictions to perform classification and other NLU tasks without any labeled or unlabeled data or human-designed verbalizers. Experiments show substantial improvements over previous zero-shot methods across diverse benchmarks, demonstrating a scalable, human-effort-free framework for zero-shot learning.

strengths.point: Fully zero-shot verbalizer induction without any labeled or unlabeled data
strengths.evidence: The method leverages only PLM static embeddings and label names to find nearest neighbors for verbalizer construction, requiring no human effort or data beyond label names.

strengths.point: Strong empirical performance across multiple NLP tasks
strengths.evidence: NPPrompt achieves +12.8% accuracy improvement on text classification and +15.6% on GLUE benchmarks compared to prior zero-shot methods, outperforming even GPT-3 on classification.

strengths.point: Flexible and scalable approach adaptable to different PLMs and tasks
strengths.evidence: The method can be applied to RoBERTa, T5, and GPT-2 architectures and extends beyond classification to multiple-choice QA without retraining or manual verbalizer design.

weaknesses.point: Lack of comparison to fully unsupervised no-data baselines
weaknesses.evidence: The evaluation omits recent zero-resource lexical matching or clustering baselines that do not require labeled or unlabeled data, limiting contextualization of the method’s performance.

weaknesses.point: No robustness analysis on label name ambiguity or multilingual settings
weaknesses.evidence: The paper does not test or discuss performance degradation when label names are ambiguous, noisy, or non-English, which challenges the core assumption of meaningful label embeddings.

weaknesses.point: No principled or adaptive strategy for neighborhood size k or weighting scheme
weaknesses.evidence: Neighborhood size k is fixed heuristically without data-driven selection, and weighting of neighbors uses only softmax-normalized cosine similarity without exploring alternatives.

weaknesses.point: Limited analysis of prompt template sensitivity and polysemy in static embeddings
weaknesses.evidence: Only a single prompt template is used, and no disambiguation strategy for polysemous label words is provided, which may reduce verbalizer quality.

weaknesses.point: No runtime, efficiency, or scalability analysis
weaknesses.evidence: Aggregating logits over multiple neighbors entails computational overhead not quantified or compared to baseline prompting methods.

weaknesses.point: Unclear handling of multiword label names and lack of error analysis
weaknesses.evidence: The approach to multiword labels is vaguely described without examples or detailed algorithms; no qualitative or error analyses are provided.

weaknesses.point: Experiments restricted to English and standard benchmarks
weaknesses.evidence: All evaluations are in English; applicability to multilingual or domain-specific label sets is untested.

questions.question: How does NPPrompt perform when label names are ambiguous, noisy, or in languages other than English?
questions.motivation: Understanding robustness to realistic label name variations is critical for practical deployment in diverse or noisy settings.

questions.question: Can adaptive or data-driven methods improve neighborhood size selection and weighting schemes for verbalizer induction?
questions.motivation: Such improvements could enhance scalability to larger, fine-grained label sets and improve verbalizer quality.

questions.question: What is the computational overhead of aggregating multiple masked token predictions compared to standard prompting or fine-tuning?
questions.motivation: Efficiency considerations are important for real-world adoption, especially on resource-constrained devices.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of comparison to fully unsupervised no-data baselines. The evaluation omits recent zero-resource lexical matching or clustering baselines that do not require labeled or unlabeled data, limiting contextualization of the method’s performance
critique_points.point_002: No robustness analysis on label name ambiguity or multilingual settings. The paper does not test or discuss performance degradation when label names are ambiguous, noisy, or non-English, which challenges the core assumption of meaningful label embeddings
critique_points.point_003: No principled or adaptive strategy for neighborhood size k or weighting scheme. Neighborhood size k is fixed heuristically without data-driven selection, and weighting of neighbors uses only softmax-normalized cosine similarity without exploring alternatives
critique_points.point_004: Limited analysis of prompt template sensitivity and polysemy in static embeddings. Only a single prompt template is used, and no disambiguation strategy for polysemous label words is provided, which may reduce verbalizer quality
critique_points.point_005: No runtime, efficiency, or scalability analysis. Aggregating logits over multiple neighbors entails computational overhead not quantified or compared to baseline prompting methods
critique_points.point_006: Unclear handling of multiword label names and lack of error analysis. The approach to multiword labels is vaguely described without examples or detailed algorithms; no qualitative or error analyses are provided
critique_points.point_007: Experiments restricted to English and standard benchmarks. All evaluations are in English; applicability to multilingual or domain-specific label sets is untested
